    // Parse the body of the message
    def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
    def slurper = new groovy.util.XmlSlurper().parseText(body)

    // Check if the specific error message is present in the body
    def errorPresent = slurper.depthFirst().find { it.name() == 'error' && it.text() == 'No results match your search criteria.' } != null

    // Set the CountObjects property based on the presence of the error message
    message.setProperty("RecordsPresent", !errorPresent)

    return message
}

 

Router : "Data?" -

A router is used to determine whether the fetched page contains any records. If records are present, the integration flow proceeds to process to next steps. However, if the page is empty, the flow terminates immediately, preventing unnecessary processing.

This check ensures optimized execution by stopping the pagination loop when there are no more records to fetch, improving efficiency and reducing unnecessary API calls.

Process Call : "Message Mapping" - 

After fetching each page, the data is passed to Message Mapping, where it is processed before being sent to the target system. This step is target-specific and is not covered in this blog.

For demonstration purposes, I have included this step to mimic the "send to target" operation after message mapping. The key focus of this blog remains on pagination handling in the Coupa adapter.

Output  / offset and limit explaintion:

For a total of 54 records, the pagination process spans 6 pages:

  • First 5 pages contain 10 records each.
  • Page 6 contains the remaining 4 records.

When the 7th page is fetched, the response contains as below, which means end of records/pages.

<errors> <error>No results match your search criteria.</error> </errors>

Page 1 : Response from the Coupa system contains 10 records as per the set limit and with offset = 0 

Page 2 : Offset = Offset (10)+ limit(10) = 20, the adapter skips the first 10 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 3 : Offset = Offset (20)+ limit(10) = 30, the adapter skips the first 20 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 4 : Offset = Offset (30)+ limit(10) = 40, the adapter skips the first 30 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 5 : Offset = Offset (40)+ limit(10) = 50, the adapter skips the first 40 records and responds with next 10 records in this call.

Page 6 : Offset = Offset (50)+ limit(10) = 60, the adapter skips the first 50 records and responds with next 10 records in this call. This is the last page containing 4 records.

Page 7 : Offset = Offset (60)+ limit(10) = 70, the adapter skips the first 50 records and responds with next 10 records in this call. <error> tag in the response which means end of records/pages

Full run in TRACE

Wrapping Up

With limit and offset handling in place, we achieve a structured and efficient pagination mechanism in Coupa Adapter. By leveraging properties like Coupa.limit and Coupa.offset, along with a controlled looping process, we ensure seamless data retrieval without unnecessary API calls.

This approach not only optimizes performance but also ensures that large datasets are processed efficiently and accurately. Stay tuned for the next blog in this series, where we continue exploring pagination strategies across different adapters! 

I hope this helps!

Cheers,

Punith

entry.title: Dynamic Interface on Cloud Integration - part 6
entry.content:
I’ll break this article into:

  • Problem/Context 
  • Our approach 
  • Git into Partner Directory 
  • Generic iflow 
  • Automated unit testing 
  • Limitations and Future plans (this link)

I hope our approach have given you some inspiration to try yourself to play around with the APIs and technology that is already there. In this final topic I would like to discuss some limitations and future goals for this interface. But first we start by what is supported already that was not discussed on this blog

  • Conversion from receiving format from JSON to XML: On many cases we realized we couldn't use the interface because we received JSON but the interface is ready just for XML, therefore we added a new parameter that once present will transform it as part of an initial step
  • Saving most parameters into custom headers: To make support life easier, we log as custom headers many of the parameters used so that they're searchable and easy to check
  • Adding static headers present on the parameter files into the dynamic: We found that not only the body but sometimes the headers need to be adapted depending on the rule, therefore we support defining different headers according to different configurations and we inject them into the process
  • Multiple http endpoints: We had one case where we had to distribute data into two or more different http urls sequentially so we incorporated that feature.
  • Multiple http adapter configurations: If you realize for HTTP adapter, you can't supply some parameters in a dynamic way (such as with SFTP), therefore we need to get all possible router for http to be able to respect whatever combination is supplied on the parameters (Proxy Type, Auth type, etc). If SAP once delivers this mechanism, we only need to change in this iflow and it will be something internal, so our expectation is that no process will be affected 

Limitations

Now we cover some limitations:

  • We support JSON as input translating into XML but we don't support converting from XML to JSON as output: We didn't have the need yet but an easy thing to incorporate
  • We don't support JSONPath or JMESPath equivalents to XPATH and JSONata or Jolt for JSON transformations: We believe they're not very known by most developers and we rather stick into XML 
  • All transformations we do are based on the current payload which might have been already transformed on previous steps of the rule execution. It can be that some of the key fields needed might not be there once you need them cause of a previous transformation: We can implement it whenever required to define on the lookup rules if the rule shall apply to the current or initially supplied XML
  • We don't support different user roles: So far only the standard ESBMessaging.send

Future plans

For future, I had an idea to implement some wizard that would create this parameters behind the scenes so that they can be prefilled in a non technical way by our business analysts.

Usually they know the formats (the source XML and the required target XML) and they even have some samples, so I developed a sample UI5 app (still in progress and almost fully generated by Chat GPT 😀), that on left side expects input files (REQ) and their respective output files (RSP). In the middle we have a chat that is determining an XSLT and executing it against all REQ and RSP files provided to assert it works for the supplied samples provided. In this case as it's in development you see the xslt in the middle doesn't comply with the files provided, therefore on the right side you see the red results.

In the end the business analysts could generate a proposal of these files into our integration team where we would just have to validate the configuration files provided and enhance them further if needed.

Of course, all this progress happens behind the scenes (mostly at weekends or at end of the day) therefore the timelines for such things will likely take time and would be experimental.

Anyway, please comment with other ideas, improvements, problems, I'll be happy to hear and improve the process on our side.

entry.title: Configure webhook notifications from SAP Cloud ALM to SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Webhook notifications allow SAP Cloud ALM (CALM) to automatically push updates to your designated endpoints whenever specific events occur. This eliminates the need for constant polling and ensures that you receive information instantly.

Example: whenever there is a change of status of a Task, CALM will trigger a webhook with notification along with basic info about the change into an iflow in SAP Cloud Integration (SCI)

Here we will set up notifications related to Tasks, but the same setup works for other resources like Requirements, Defects and so on. 
The configuration needs to be done at 4 different places:

  • Create webhook receiving iflow (SCI)
  • Set Endpoint in Landscape Management (CALM)
  • Create Webhook and Subscription in External API Management (CALM)
  • Assign Subscription to Project in Implementation (CALM)

Create webhook receiving iflow

Create a simple iflow with HTTPS Sender adapter, which will serve as the notification endpoint. Deploy it, so that the URL is available. It can be something like like https://[tenant_address]. hana.ondemand.com/http/tasks_notifications
Note down the path /http/tasks_notifications, or whatever you named it, as this we will use later.

Endpoint in Landscape Management

Navigate to Administration -> Landscape Management and select the system to which you want to send your notifications, in our case it will be SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration).
Go to Endpoint tab and create an endpoint with Use Case External API Management.

To be able to set the authentication, you need to create a Service Key in BTP Cockpit. Copy-paste the credential information into the Endpoint configuration and now you have a working connection to SCI.

Create Webhook and Subscription in External API Management

Navigate to Administration -> External API Management. On the left pane you can see Subscriptions management and Webhooks management. Go to Webhooks management, as we first create a Webhook and then assign it to Subscription.

Proceed with configuration as per the screenshot. LMS Service and Endpoint is what we set up in previous step. As Path set the path of our notification iflow from step 1. No need to specify the full URL, as that will be taken from the Endpoint.

Ext. Resource Type determines the type of resource we want to be notified about. Nevertheless, the same webhook can be used for other resources as well.

Now let's create Subscription. Just toggle onto Subscriptions management on the left pane, create a Subscription and assign the Webhook, which we created before. Again, follow the screenshot. 

Assign Subscription to Project in Implementation

Navigate to Implementation -> Projects and Setup and select your project.
It is important to point out that you can set up notifications per Project. You can be notified by changes on Tasks belonging into one Project, but Tasks of other Project will not trigger anything.

Go to Subscriptions tab and there you can activate notifications for individual Item Type. See that we can assign the same Subscription to all Item Types, regardless of what Resource Type we selected in previous step. 

Test our notifications

Now everything is set up. Go to any Task within the Project, for which we activated notifications and perform a change, e.g. switch it to another status.
Navigate to Administration -> External API Management and select Console from the left pane. Refresh if necessary and you will see all your webhook traffic.
If everything has been properly configured, you also received the payload into your SCI notification iflow.

entry.title: Automated Undeployment and deployment of selective iflows during planned downtime
entry.content:
During planned system downtime or in case of emergency downtime, we require all iflows to be undeployed at the start of downtime and then redeploy them again when the downtime is over. This task usually is performed manually and requires a dedicated person to be available to perform the activity. There are blogs created by fellow community members which provide solution to deploy-undeploy all iflows. However, there are cases where all iflows or artifacts need not be undeployed. For eg: in case of S4HANA downtime, only iflows inbound to S4HANA need to be undeployed while outbound ones, value mapping, script collections etc. do not require to be undeployed.

This blog provides detailed steps and components that are required to manage only selective iflows during downtime.

Un Deployment Process

The first step of setting up this functionality is to identify the list of iflows which are needed to be undeployed during outage. 

This list can be maintained in an excel file or notepad file with each Iflow ID in a separate line.

(i) Create a simple iflow to write the iflow list to a datastore. This entry in datastore should be a one-time activity unless there is a change in the list of iflows to be deployed or undeployed.

(ii) Un Deployment Process

Create an iflow to initiate the undeployment process.

 

Firstly, we initialize a datastore (FailedIflowList) with blank payload for storing the list of iflows which fail to undeploy in later processing.

Now we load of list of iflows that need to be undeployed from Datastore (IflowList) that was loaded in the first step.

This iflow list will be stored in a property which will be used later for triggering email for the final status of undeployment.

Create an XSD like below which can be used for converting the CSV payload into XML format.

Use a csv to xml convert to convert the CSV payload into XML using the XSD created above.

Use General Splitter to split each Iflow entry into individual xml which will be passed to local integration process.

Now, local subprocess call is made to execute the undeployment process.

 

Fetch CSRF token from Cloud Integration URL using below logic

(a) Set x-csrf-token=fetch in header of the content modifier.

(b) use a Request-Reply to fetch the CSRF token by calling Cloud Integration URL 

Reset the split payload as body and then extract the iflow ID from xpath and set it as property.

Perform HTTP call to CPI endpoint URL as shown below to undeploy the iflow

Check if the undeployment call was successful

If successful, end the subprocess. Else, get the list of failed iflows from datastore (FailedIflowList) which was initialized earlier. For first record, the list will be empty but in further iterations, it might have some records. Now append this artifact id to the list and rewrite back to datastore.  

Once the control is passed to the main integration process, it checks whether the last record has been processed by checking for condition ${property.CamelSplitComplete} = 'true'

Once the last record from the iflow list has been processed, we read the datastore FailedIflowList if any entries are present. If no entry is found, a success email is sent to stakeholders notifying that the process is successful.

In case of failure, a process completion notification along with the list of failed iflows is sent to them so that they can take necessary action accordingly.

Deployment Process

The deployment process is almost identical to the undeployment process except the endpoint and method that we use for deploying the iflow.

Firstly, we initialize a datastore (FailedIflowList) with blank payload for storing the list of iflows which fail to undeploy in later processing.

Now we load of list of iflows that need to be undeployed from Datastore (IflowList)

This iflow list will be stored in a property which will be used later for triggering email for the final status of deployment.

Use the same xsd that we created in undeployment process for converting the CSV payload into XML format.

Use General Splitter to split each Iflow entry into individual xml which will be passed to local integration process.

Now, local subprocess call is made to execute the deployment process.

fetch CSRF token from Cloud Integration URL using below logic

(a) Set x-csrf-token=fetch in header of the content modifier.

(b) use a Request-Reply to fetch the CSRF token by calling Cloud Integration URL 

Set the response header set-cookie in an exchange property cookie.

set header cookie after formatting in groovy script as shown below

Reset the split payload as body and then extract the iflow ID from xpath and set it as property.

Perform HTTP call to CPI endpoint URL as shown below to deploy the iflow

Check if the deployment is successful.

If successful, end the subprocess. Else, get the list of failed iflows from datastore (FailedIflowList) which was initialized earlier. For first record, the list will be empty but in further iterations, it might have some records. Now append this artifact id to the list and rewrite back to datastore.

Once the control is passed to the main integration process, it checks whether the last record has been processed by checking for condition ${property.CamelSplitComplete} = 'true'

Once the last record from the iflow list has been processed, we read the datastore FailedIflowList if any entries are present. If no entry is found, a success email is sent to stakeholders notifying that the process is successful.

In case of failure, a process completion notification along with the list of failed iflows is sent to them so that they can take necessary action accordingly.

entry.title: Blog series on my CPI + Camel learning journey - Part2 - Camel in CPI
entry.content:
Link back to index: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/blog-series-on-my-cpi-camel-learning-journey/ba-p/14031053

Intro

First of all, Camel development to me is almost synonymous with Java development.
In a way, that it seems natural, that you have to be Java developer to do Camel properly. 
So keeping this in mind you might want to check proper official "Getting started guide" (and yes, camel app can be a real standalone Java application!)
 
But here we will first focus on abstractions Camel provides, and then try mapping it to CPI world (or the other way around) as we will do our best to keep away from any programming (including groovy) to follow the low-code paradigm.
 
Ideally, understanding of how "java-world-camel" really works under the hood could lead us (with help of our capic tool) to some insights of how we could achieve more in "low-code-world-cpi" (or maybe things we should avoid).

What do we have in Camel

So on this figure we see architecture overview
Not much to comment on, but that leaves us with following main abstractions:
  • CamelContext 
  • Route
  • Endpoint + Component
  • Processor
 
So far no Messages though!
Ok, lets try this one about CamelContext and see where we start having them maybe.
I strongly recommend thoroughly reading it through!
Here's the excerpt helping us to define entities we encountered earlier:
  1. The CamelContext is the runtime system, which holds together all the fundamental concepts of Apache Camel (Routes, Endpoints, Components, etc).
  2. Routes are a core abstraction for Camel. The simplest way to define a route is as a chain of Processors.
  3. To wire Processors and Endpoints together to form Routes, Camel defines a DSL.
  4. The Processor is a core Camel concept that represents a node capable of using, creating, or modifying an incoming Exchange.
 
Basically, it just so happened that CPI uses blueprint xml DSL to convert iflows into, thus giving us an option to play with all that stuff locally. But the "real one" is Java DSL of course.
 
And then there is really important section about Processors finally introducing Exchange and Message
There we read: "During routing, Exchanges flow from one processor to another; as such, you can think of a Route as a graph having specialized Processors as the nodes, and lines that connect the output of one Processor to the input of another."
 
And also: "At each Processor step, the output (Out) Message from the previous step is the input (In) Message of the next. In many cases, processors don’t set an Out Message, so in this case the In Message is reused."
 
Let's also have another figure from Red Hat Fuse guide also depicting Exchange flowing through the route.
There they define "Exchange object is a wrapper that encapsulates a received Message and stores its associated metadata (including the exchange properties). In addition, if the current Message is dispatched to a producer Endpoint, the Exchange provides a temporary slot to hold the reply (the Out Message)."
 
So now finally we see what's going on here: Exchange object is passed around, and Messages can be, well, exchanged by processors, so that next one receives what the previous returned (potentially NEW message).
 
To me it was confusing as in CPI they don't introduce us to this concept of "actual exchange" of messages (in plural), and this can mislead us to conceptualizing that both Exchange and Message are "singletons"
 
But here's some more info from this faq regarding In and Out Messages to clarify it finally:

  • The Out Message from each step is used as the In Message for the next step

  • if there is no Out Message then the In Message is used instead (!!!)

  • For the InOut MEP the Out from the last step in the route is returned to the producer. In case of InOnly the last Out is thrown away

Here InOut and InOnly MEP can be thought of as synchronous (sender waits for last Out message) and asynchronous communication. It is technically just a flag on Exchange though.
 
And finally, we might need something like this stolen from sap press blogs (who knows, maybe their book explains everything I am writing about in my blog series here) to illustrate Exchange to Messages relations.
 
So, key takeaways here are that Headers and Attachments are "attached" to a particular Message object (as of course Body is) that can be lost/exchanged, while Properties are on Exchange level.
Meaning, in theory, you can not rely on the fact that Headers and Attachments you add to your Message will "last" until the end of processing, while (exchange) Properties should.
Also don't forget that Out message (if processor returns it) becomes In one next step.
 

In short

So, to conclude this section let me try putting it all together in one sentence in a form of "The House That Jack Built":
  • Here's Message (having Body and Headers) we received through the Endpoint bound to some Component,
  • And transferred between chain of Processors (to be modified or exchanged) via and Exchange object (having it's own Properties)
  • Flowing through one or more Routes (defined in certain DSL) belonging to a certain CamelContext (sometimes separate)
  • Running in some OSGI runtime (thus "communicating" to other bundles) Apache built.
 
Basically, it means while there are some processors to process the exchange, camel route engine will pass it further down the graph.
The last processor to be called (not only within current route) is literally the end of the route processing.
 

What do we have in CPI

In CPI we have some other abstractions as iflows are modelled in BPMN notation.

So let's now try to take a look at CPI official documentation to find some hints at how it is mapped to Camel afterwards

 

Here's the quote:

"A graphical editor allows you, the integration developer, to model the message processing steps and specify in detail what happens to the message during processing.

In detail, you define the following aspects in an integration flow:

  • The senders and receivers of the message
  • How the senders and receivers are connected to the tenant (adapters)
  • The steps that define the message processing"
 
Ok.. not so informative, but at least this is something reminding us the Chain of Processors.
What if we now assume the following:
  • Adapters are camel Components (that are responsible for implementation of Endpoints logic)
  • Steps are camel Processors
  • Message is, well, camel Message (in most cases, In Message)
Next, we have another article in docs explaining "Camel Data Model"
 
Here we find that indeed "An integration flow is a BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation)-like model that allows you to specify how a message is to be processed on a tenant."
 
And also that "To interpret an integration flow model at runtime, it is transformed into an XML structure that is compatible with Apache Camel, an Open Source integration framework for Java that supports the mediation and routing of messages of any format."
 
What that really means is, our BPMN iflow becomes one or more camel routes within same camel context defined in blueprint xml dsl.
 
And yes, indeed they agree that we have Exchange object in place: "An Exchange can be seen as an abstraction of a message exchange process as it is executed by the Camel framework. An Exchange is identified uniquely by an Exchange ID. In the Properties area of the Exchange, additional data can be stored temporarily during message processing. This data is available for the runtime during the whole duration of the message exchange."
 

So what becomes what?

Let's now try to figure out how other elements of an iflow are mapped into camel model.
 
Well, it would be a good guess to think that Integration Process "shape" becomes a Route.
The same actually is correct for Local Integration Process.
But with Exception Subprocess it's not that easy - it actually becomes embedded in our route (we'll cover that later)
 
Another major difference would be that events (Start, End, Error, Escalation) also do NOT exist in Camel.
Though Start has at least semantics of from in camel world (as we connect our Sender adapter to it)
 
Other stuff would be more or less closely related to so-called EIPs
For example, Content Modifier would be a combination of setBody, setHeader, setProperty eips.
 
We're not aiming to cover it all here now (maybe later I'll do a separate post with some comparison table), but it is important to understand that on each step in iflow there is either a camel eip processor (eg router step becomes choice) or component/endpoint (eg <camel:toD uri="sap-map-pi..."/> for message mapping step).
// OK, sometimes like in case of HTTP receiver (ahc component, and I guess exactly because of Out Message existence) this becomes more complicated, so it is NOT a direct 1 to 1 mapping.
 
But what about receivers?
Well, again, no such thing in Camel, therefore we only have to (or toD) when we either define our Receiver as "final" step after "End Message" or when doing "Request Reply", or doing "Process Direct" and "Local Process" call - the only difference would be the Endpoint: direct (to route inside our Context), direct-vm (to route outisde our Context, but sap uses its own sap-processdirect) or some adapter like http or odata.
Anyway, in our route blueprint we will see something like <camel:to uri="..." />
 
This is VERY important to understand, and here's why.
As we said above, route is just a chain of processors we pass our exchange object to.
We only reach the end of that route when the last processor is called.
Now imagine we have an HTTP sender as we have in our simplest "Sender-initiated scenario with external data source and receiver"
 
It looks like this
HTTP adapter sets InOut MEP, which is not only synchronous in nature, but also means we shall respond with what the last message in chain is.
So, what would be return?
From what we see here it woud make sense to assume that we just return what our webshop odata call returns us, right? WRONG!
Why? Well, because we actually have something like at the end of this route:
<camel:to uri="sap-processdirect:foo?address=/custom/GenericReceiverDataStore"/>
So we kinda have something called "Message End", but does it stop camel from processing the exchange as usual? Of course, not!
Meaning, we actually will respond with what THIS guy will finish processing.
What I encourage you to do is to put Content Modifier where I have a red circle and set some random body.
And guess what? - you will see THAT body in your response from the Sender-Initiated scenario endpoint.
Again, this is exactly how Camel routing works, and this is exactly because of InOut (synchronous handling).
 

So what?

Well, I'd say, the biggest takeaway from this example is: in case you have InOut processing and don't change it to InOnly later (for example, placing messages into JMS queue, of which we have limited supply in CPI ironically), the whole processing is synchronous AND in-memory.
And that makes it oh-so-different from what you might read in this book where Message oriented architecture is Asynchronous (queue-oriented by nature).
This is where this BPMN low-code approach seems somewhat misleading to me in regards to EIP, ESB and all those enterprise level message oriented architectures: while it is possible to implement proper ESB on CPI, looks like it is NOT intended for really complex scenarions (eg cases like this with distributed transactions which camel supports while CPI does not even mention anywhere).
 

Moving on to Exceptions

As we said earlier, there is no such thing as Excepion Subprocess in camel, but there are onException clauses we can put to properly handle exceptions (who would expect that in java world)...
But then the question would be, how does End/Error/Escalation magic work?
Well, for those 3 examples from guidelines I made a new one almost identical to Sender-Initiated scenario we saw earlier.
It looks like this:
The main difference from this one is we don't use script step to "simulate exception" (why this even makes sense?..), we just instead rely on the fact that http adapter will throw one in case of 4xx/5xx answers.
In our case we will try reading Product by key, so it's trivial to trigger 404 here (which is also a rather realistic scenario, I'd say).
So, is there a way to see what does the camel route look like?
Yes, there is, and in our demo for this part I'll show it in action. Here are the screenshots of camel context outline feature we have in capic to quickly check routes and from/to uris we have.
So, here's how our End, Error, and Escalation events look like (respectively):

In all cases we see that "success path" goes to our Receiver, while inside onException things start looking weird.

Whats going on there?

Well, our outline only shows us part of the picture of course, but at least we can see that Escalation case is different as message is sent to sap-alert (which explains why you have some generic error in response).

How are End and Error cases are different though?

Well, with this another tool will gladly help us - git!

First picture is a diff between End and Error, while the second one - between Error and Escalation

Here we see <camel:handled> changed from true to false and also that sap-specific exchange property 

"SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed"=false is removed (meaning it is true by default)
Here we see that  <camel:handled> changed back from false to true and that <camel:to uri="sap-alert://escalation..."/> receiver showed up.

Basically, thats it: <camel:handled> just signals to Camel itself if route was able to handle the exception (and in the demo I'll show what happens when our local ftp sender case fails to do so), while some custom exchange property ("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed") or any additional receiver ("sap-alert://escalation") does not make any difference  to Camel runtime.

And hence, this is how our local blueprint outline would look like:

In both cases we send message to our Generic Receiver, and the only difference is if we mark <camel:handled> as true or not.

Next Demo

Basically, we just modify our Exception Subprocess scenarion on CPI side, then sync it to local git and make local blueprints to compare them to each other.

It ended up to be a pretty long one though...

00:00 Intro and quick initial setup

03:28 Rezip bundles and test sample iflow (remote and local)

09:03 Sync iflow from CPI to git and make new local blueprint for End message case

21:07 Change iflow to Error event and sync and make local blueprint again

31:22 Change iflow to Escalation event and sync and make local blueprint again

40:13 (Unplanned) Revert iflow to End message and make a final conclusion regarding local blueprints equivalence

entry.title: Machine Translation in the Documentation for SAP Integration Suite & SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello, integration community!

Exciting news just dropped — machine translation is now available for the documentation of SAP Integration Suite and Cloud Integration on SAP Help Portal! This feature brings us one step closer to giving you seamless access to all the resources you need, no matter what language you speak.

Why This Matters

If you’ve ever found yourself navigating documentation in a language you’re not entirely comfortable with, you know what a game-changer this is. With machine translation at your fingertips, you can easily understand complex instructions, learn best practices, and troubleshoot issues without any linguistic hurdles. Whether you're trying to integrate new systems, troubleshoot an API issue, or simply understand the latest features, having documentation in your preferred language can make all the difference.

How It Works

Wondering how this new feature works? It’s simple! The machine translation tool harnesses cutting-edge technology to provide real-time translations of our docs into multiple languages. Sure, no machine translation can be perfect, but we’re confident it’ll make your life easier.

You can try it right now: Go to the documentation for SAP Integration Suite or SAP Cloud Integration and next to the title, choose your preferred language from a list of additional languages.

Your Feedback Matters

We understand that machine translations might not always be flawless. That’s why we’ve made it easy for you to provide feedback: If you spot an error or feel a translation could be improved, let us know in our survey. You can find it in the disclaimer at the top of the page or the side bar in the Help Portal. Your input will help us refine the machine translation and ensure it meets everyone's needs.

We’re thrilled to see how machine translation can enhance your experience with SAP Integration Suite and SAP Cloud Integration. So, go ahead, give it a try, and don’t hesitate to share your thoughts with us!

entry.title: Integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys via SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post provides a step-by-step guide on integrating SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the SAP Integration Suite. The focus is on setting up a straightforward integration flow (iFlow) to extract contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2 and create corresponding contacts in SAP Emarsys.

The goal is to demonstrate how to connect both systems, using APIs and middleware to automate data transfer. This serves as a foundation for more complex integrations in the future, making it easier to extend the process with additional functionality. A key aspect of this integration is OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, which is required to securely access SAP Emarsys. We will cover how authentication works in this context and how to configure it properly within the iFlow.

 

Prerequisites

Before implementing this iFlow ensure that you have the following:

  • An active SAP Integration Suite tenant with Cloud Integration enabled
  • Access to a SAP Sales Cloud V2 account with API permissions
  • Access to an SAP Emarsys account with API permissions

 

Implementation

Overview of the Integration Flow

Overview of the iFlow between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using SAP Integration SuiteThe diagram above provides an overview of the iFlow, which connects SAP Sales Cloud V2 with SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. This process automates retrieving contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, processes the information, and transfers it to SAP Emarsys to create corresponding contacts.

  1. Start Timer – Initiates the iFlow execution at predefined intervals.
  2. Set Filter – Applies conditions to select only relevant contacts from SAP Sales Cloud V2.
  3. Get Sales Cloud Contacts – Fetches contact data via the SAP Sales Cloud API.
  4. Extract Sales Cloud Contacts – Processes and structures the extracted data.
  5. Configure SAP Emarsys Request –Prepares the Request and Setting Up OAuth Authentication
  6. Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys – Sends the contact data via an API request to SAP Emarsys.

In the following sections, each step of the iFlow will be explained in detail.

Step 1: Start Timer - Triggering the Integration Flow

Timer Configuration – Scheduling the iFlow to run at regular intervals

The iFlow is initiated by a timer-based trigger, which ensures that contact data is regularly synchronized between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys.

As shown in the screenshot, the timer is configured using a Simple Schedule, where the iFlow runs every hour between February 27, 2025, at 09:00 UTC and March 6, 2025, at 18:00 UTC.

Step 2: Set Filter - Filtering Relevant Contacts

Configure SAP Sales Cloud V2 Request

In this step, we apply a filter to ensure that only the necessary contacts are fetched from SAP Sales Cloud V2. First, we limit the selection to contacts associated with the United States. Second, we include only those that have been newly created or updated within the last hour.

After adding the timer to trigger the iFlow, the next step is to insert a Content Modifier to define the query parameters for retrieving the relevant contact data.

  1. Add a Content Modifier
  2. Navigate to the Message Header tab.
  3. Create a new header entry:
    • Action: Create
    • Name: CamelHttpQuery (you can choose your own name)
    • Source Type: Expression
    • Source Value: $filter=isContactPersonFor/country eq 'US' and (adminData/createdOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z' or adminData/updatedOn gt '${date:now:yyyy-MM-dd}T${date:now:HH}:00:00Z'})

The filter dynamically adjusts the timestamp to the beginning of the current hour, ensuring that each execution of the integration flow retrieves only the latest contact records.

Step 3: Get Sales Cloud Contacts - Fetching Data from SAP Sales Cloud V2

To retrieve contact data from SAP Sales Cloud V2, we need to set up a Request Reply and connect it to an HTTP Receiver Adapter. This ensures that the iFlow sends a request to SAP Sales Cloud V2 and processes the response dynamically.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

The Request Reply step is required to handle synchronous communication with SAP Sales Cloud. It ensures that the request is sent, and the response is processed before moving to the next step in the iFlow.

  1. Add a Request Reply step after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply step to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

The HTTP Receiver Adapter is responsible for sending the request to SAP Sales Cloud V2. The configuration is as follows:

Configure the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2

  • Address: The endpoint for retrieving contact persons: 
    https://mytenant.crm.cloud.sap/sap/c4c/api/v1/contact-person-service/contactPersons
  • Method: GET is selected to fetch data.
  • Proxy Type: Set to Internet, ensuring external API communication.
  • Authentication: Basic
  • Credentials: Since SAP Sales Cloud V2 requires authentication, we must configure Basic Authentication in the Security Material section of the SAP Integration Suite.
    • Set up Basic Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite: Go to "Monitor" → "Security Material" → Create a new "User Credentials" → enter a Credential Name, Username and Password → add the Credential Name in HTTP Receiver Adapter

The dynamic filter query that was set up in the Content Modifier is passed automatically in the request to ensure only relevant contact data is retrieved.

Step 4: Extract Sales Cloud Contacts - Extracting Contact Information

In this step, we process the contact data retrieved from SAP Sales Cloud V2, extract the relevant fields, and format them for further use in the integration. To achieve this, we use a Groovy script that structures the data according to SAP Emarsys' requirements. For simplicity, this example extracts only the first name, last name, and email address, but additional fields can be included as needed.

 

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper;
import groovy.json.JsonOutput;

def Message processData(Message message) {
    def body = message.getBody(String)  
    def jsonSlurper = new JsonSlurper()
    def jsonResponse = jsonSlurper.parseText(body)  

    if (!jsonResponse || !jsonResponse.value || jsonResponse.value.isEmpty()) {
        throw new RuntimeException("No contact data found. API Response: " + body)
    }

    def contactsList = jsonResponse.value.collect { contact ->
        return [
            "3": contact.eMail ?: "",  
            "1": contact.givenName ?: "",  
            "2": contact.familyName ?: ""  
        ]
    }

    def emarsysPayload = [
        key_id: "3",
        contacts: contactsList
    ]

    message.setProperty("EmarsysBody", JsonOutput.toJson(emarsysPayload))  // Store entire JSON in a property

    return message;
}

 

We start by retrieving the API response as a string and parsing it into a JSON object. If no contacts are found, we throw an exception to prevent unnecessary API calls.

Next, we extract the key attributes needed for SAP Emarsys. In this example, we focus on:

  • Email Address (3)
  • First Name (1)
  • Last Name (2)

We iterate over the contact list and map each contact’s attributes into a structured format. If any of these fields are missing, we assign an empty string to ensure consistency in the data structure.

Once we have extracted the required fields, we structure the data into a JSON payload that SAP Emarsys can process. Since SAP Emarsys uses the email address as the unique identifier, we set the key_id to 3

Finally, we store the structured JSON in a message property (EmarsysBody), making it available for the next step in the iFlow.

Step 5: Configure SAP Emarsys Request

In this step, we prepare the data extracted from SAP Sales Cloud V2 for submission to the SAP Emarsys API. This involves setting up a Content Modifier and configuring the necessary OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication using the Integration Suite.

1. Add a Content Modifier

First, add a Content Modifier step in the iFlow after the Groovy script. The purpose of this step is to store the payload that will be sent to the SAP Emarsys API.

Before creating the new headers, delete the existing CamelHttpQuery header to avoid conflicts with the new request configuration. To do this, add a new row in the Message Header section and select Delete as the action. Enter CamelHttpQuery as the header name and leave the other fields empty.

Message Header

HTTP headers for Emarsys request and OAuth authentication

Next, we need to add the required headers for the OAuth 2.0 request and data exchange with the SAP Emarsys API:

  • Go to the Message Header tab.
  • Add first header:
    • Name: Content-Type
    • Source Type: Constant
    • Source Value: application/json
  • Add second header:
    • Name: Accept
    • Source Type: Constant
    • Source Value: application/json
Message Body

Next, we need to define the message body that will be sent to the SAP Emarsys API. This body contains the structured contact data extracted and formatted in the Groovy script:

  • Navigate to the Message Body tab.
  • Type: Expression 
  • Body: ${property.EmarsysBody} - we set this property in the groovy script.

2. Configure OAuth 2.0 Authentication

To connect to the SAP Emarsys API, you need to configure OAuth 2.0 authentication in the Integration Suite. This is done by setting up OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials in the Security Material.

Configuring Security Material for Emarsys Authentication

You can obtain the OAuth 2.0 credentials directly from SAP Emarsys or configure them using OpenID Connect with SAP Cloud Identity. For detailed steps on setting up credentials via OpenID Connect, refer to the official  SAP Emarsys documentation here.

Alternatively, you can create API credentials directly in SAP Emarsys. You can find the detailed steps here. Ensure that the Token Service URL is correctly set to the SAP Emarsys token endpoint.

When configuring OAuth 2.0 credentials in the Security Material of the Integration Suite, make sure to populate the SCOPE field (e.g.'api'). Missing scope values can lead to authentication errors. For more details on setting up OAuth 2.0 authentication, refer to this SAP Community blog.

Step 6: Create Contacts in SAP Emarsys

In this step, we create new contacts in SAP Emarsys using the extracted and formatted data from SAP Sales Cloud V2. To achieve this, we need to configure a Request Reply Step and an HTTP Receiver Adapter to send the contact data to SAP Emarsys.

1. Setting Up the Request Reply Step

  1. Add a Request Reply after the Content Modifier.
  2. Connect the Request Reply to an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

2. Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

  • Address: The endpoint for creating contacts in SAP Emarsys: https://api.emarsys.net/api/v3/contact
  • Proxy Type: Internet
  • Method: POST
  • Authentication: OAuth2 Client Credentials
  • Credential Name: Your credential name from the previous step

At this point, the contact data extracted from SAP Sales Cloud V2 is ready to be created in SAP Emarsys. The HTTP Receiver Adapter sends the formatted data as a POST request to the SAP Emarsys API.

 

Validating the Integration

After setting up the iFlow and configuring all necessary steps, we can now test the integration to confirm that contact data is successfully transferred from SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Emarsys.

Step 1: Create a Contact in SAP Sales Cloud V2

To validate the integration, we start by creating a new contact in SAP Sales Cloud V2. In this example, a contact named Emma Evans with the email address emma.evans@alpha.cx.demo.sap was created in SAP Sales Cloud V2.

Step 2: Verify That the Contact is Not Yet Available in SAP Emarsys

Next, we search for the newly created contact in SAP Emarsys using the email address. As shown in the screenshot, the search returns no results, confirming that the contact is not yet present in SAP Emarsys.

Step 3: Execute the Integration Flow

We then manually trigger the iFlow to push the newly created contact from SAP Sales Cloud V2 to SAP Emarsys. The iFlow sends a POST request to the SAP Emarsys API with the contact details extracted and formatted in the previous steps.

Step 4: Confirm That the Contact is Now Available in SAP Emarsys

Finally, we search for the contact in SAP Emarsys again using the email address. This time, the contact is successfully found, confirming that the data transfer was successful. The contact's first name, last name, and email address are correctly displayed in SAP Emarsys, indicating that the iFlow is working as expected.

 

Conclusion

In this post, we walked through the process of setting up an end-to-end integration between SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys using the Cloud Integration component within the SAP Integration Suite. We started by configuring the connection to SAP Sales Cloud V2, filtering the data to retrieve only relevant contacts, and extracting the necessary fields using a Groovy script. We then prepared the data for SAP Emarsys, set up OpenID Connect (OIDC, OAuth 2.0, JWT) based API authentication, and sent the data to SAP Emarsys using an HTTP Receiver Adapter.

The successful execution of the integration was confirmed when the contact created in SAP Sales Cloud V2 appeared in SAP Emarsys after the process ran. This demonstrates how easily the SAP Integration Suite can be used to connect SAP Sales Cloud V2 and SAP Emarsys - laying the foundation for more complex processes in the future. 

By following these steps, you can build a similar integration in your environment, adapting the filter conditions and field mapping to meet your specific business needs.
